title: Stomach

infobox.value: [Right gastric artery](/wiki/Right_gastric_artery "Right gastric artery"), [left gastric artery](/wiki/Left_gastric_artery "Left gastric artery"), [right gastro-omental artery](/wiki/Right_gastro-omental_artery "Right gastro-omental artery"), [left gastro-omental artery](/wiki/Left_gastro-omental_artery "Left gastro-omental artery"), [short gastric arteries](/wiki/Short_gastric_arteries "Short gastric arteries")

[Right gastric vein](/wiki/Right_gastric_vein "Right gastric vein"), [left gastric vein](/wiki/Left_gastric_vein "Left gastric vein"), [right gastroepiploic vein](/wiki/Right_gastroepiploic_vein "Right gastroepiploic vein"), [left gastroepiploic vein](/wiki/Left_gastroepiploic_vein "Left gastroepiploic vein"), [short gastric veins](/wiki/Short_gastric_veins "Short gastric veins")

is a muscular, [hollow organ](/wiki/Hollow_organ "Hollow organ") in the [upper gastrointestinal tract](/wiki/Upper_gastrointestinal_tract "Upper gastrointestinal tract") of [humans](/wiki/Human "Human") and many other animals, including several [invertebrates](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate"). The [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") name for the stomach is *gaster* which is used as *gastric* in medical terms related to the stomach.[[3]](#cite_note-MW1-3) The stomach has a dilated structure and functions as a vital organ in the [digestive system](/wiki/Digestive_system "Digestive system"). The stomach is involved in the [gastric phase of digestion](/wiki/Gastric_phase "Gastric phase"), following the [cephalic phase](/wiki/Cephalic_phase "Cephalic phase") in which the sight and smell of food and the act of [chewing](/wiki/Chewing "Chewing") are stimuli. In the stomach a chemical breakdown of food takes place by means of secreted [digestive enzymes](/wiki/Digestive_enzyme "Digestive enzyme") and [gastric acid](/wiki/Gastric_acid "Gastric acid"). It also plays a role in regulating gut [microbiota](/wiki/Microbiota "Microbiota"), influencing digestion and overall health.[[4]](#cite_note-Chaurasia2013-4)

is where the contents of the esophagus empty from the [gastroesophageal sphincter](/wiki/Gastroesophageal_sphincter "Gastroesophageal sphincter") into the cardiac orifice, the opening into the gastric cardia.[[11]](#cite_note-SCHWARTZ2010-11)[[10]](#cite_note-Elsevier-10) A cardiac notch at the left of the cardiac orifice, marks the beginning of the [greater curvature](/wiki/Greater_curvature "Greater curvature") of the stomach. A horizontal line across from the cardiac notch gives the dome-shaped region called the fundus.[[10]](#cite_note-Elsevier-10) The cardia is a very small region of the stomach that surrounds the esophageal opening.[[10]](#cite_note-Elsevier-10)
(from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") 'bottom') is formed in the upper curved part.[[12]](#cite_note-SEER2025-12)
or **corpus** is the main, central region of the stomach.[[12]](#cite_note-SEER2025-12)

The **stomach** is a muscular, [hollow organ](/wiki/Hollow_organ "Hollow organ") in the [upper gastrointestinal tract](/wiki/Upper_gastrointestinal_tract "Upper gastrointestinal tract") of [humans](/wiki/Human "Human") and many other animals, including several [invertebrates](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate"). The [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") name for the stomach is *gaster* which is used as *gastric* in medical terms related to the stomach.[[3]](#cite_note-MW1-3) The stomach has a dilated structure and functions as a vital organ in the [digestive system](/wiki/Digestive_system "Digestive system"). The stomach is involved in the [gastric phase of digestion](/wiki/Gastric_phase "Gastric phase"), following the [cephalic phase](/wiki/Cephalic_phase "Cephalic phase") in which the sight and smell of food and the act of [chewing](/wiki/Chewing "Chewing") are stimuli. In the stomach a chemical breakdown of food takes place by means of secreted [digestive enzymes](/wiki/Digestive_enzyme "Digestive enzyme") and [gastric acid](/wiki/Gastric_acid "Gastric acid"). It also plays a role in regulating gut [microbiota](/wiki/Microbiota "Microbiota"), influencing digestion and overall health.[[4]](#cite_note-Chaurasia2013-4)

The stomach is located between the [esophagus](/wiki/Esophagus "Esophagus") and the [small intestine](/wiki/Small_intestine "Small intestine"). The [pyloric sphincter](/wiki/Pyloric_sphincter "Pyloric sphincter") controls the passage of partially digested food ([chyme](/wiki/Chyme "Chyme")) from the stomach into the [duodenum](/wiki/Duodenum "Duodenum"), the first and shortest part of the small intestine, where [peristalsis](/wiki/Peristalsis "Peristalsis") takes over to move this through the rest of the intestines.

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structure")]

In the [human digestive system](/wiki/Human_digestive_system "Human digestive system"), the stomach lies between the [esophagus](/wiki/Esophagus "Esophagus") and the [duodenum](/wiki/Duodenum "Duodenum") (the first part of the [small intestine](/wiki/Small_intestine "Small intestine")). It is in the [left upper quadrant](/wiki/Quadrants_and_regions_of_abdomen#Quadrants "Quadrants and regions of abdomen") of the [abdominal cavity](/wiki/Abdominal_cavity "Abdominal cavity"). The top of the stomach lies against the [diaphragm](/wiki/Diaphragm_(anatomy) "Diaphragm (anatomy)"). Lying behind the stomach is the [pancreas](/wiki/Pancreas "Pancreas"). A large double fold of visceral [peritoneum](/wiki/Peritoneum "Peritoneum") called the [greater omentum](/wiki/Greater_omentum "Greater omentum") hangs down from the [greater curvature](/wiki/Curvatures_of_the_stomach "Curvatures of the stomach") of the stomach. Two [sphincters](/wiki/Sphincter "Sphincter") keep the contents of the stomach contained; the [lower esophageal sphincter](/wiki/Oesophagus#Sphincters "Oesophagus") (found in the cardiac region), at the junction of the esophagus and stomach, and the [pyloric sphincter](/wiki/Pyloric_sphincter "Pyloric sphincter") at the junction of the stomach with the duodenum.

The stomach is surrounded by [parasympathetic](/wiki/Parasympathetic_nervous_system "Parasympathetic nervous system") (inhibitor) and [sympathetic](/wiki/Sympathetic_nervous_system "Sympathetic nervous system") (stimulant) [plexuses](/wiki/Plexuses "Plexuses") (networks of [blood vessels](/wiki/Blood_vessel "Blood vessel") and [nerves](/wiki/Nerve "Nerve") in the [anterior](/wiki/Anterior "Anterior") gastric, [posterior](/wiki/Posterior_(anatomy) "Posterior (anatomy)"), [superior](/wiki/Anatomical_terms_of_location#Superior_and_inferior "Anatomical terms of location") and [inferior](/wiki/Anatomical_terms_of_location#Superior_and_inferior "Anatomical terms of location"), celiac and myenteric), which regulate both the secretory activity of the stomach and the motor (motion) activity of its muscles.

The stomach is [distensible](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/distensibility "wikt:distensibility"), and can normally expand to hold about one litre of food.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The shape of the stomach depends upon the degree of its distension and that of surrounding viscera, e.g. the colon.[[4]](#cite_note-Chaurasia2013-4) When empty, the stomach is somewhat J-shaped; when partially distended, it becomes [pyriform](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/pyriform "wikt:pyriform") in shape. In obese persons, it is more horizontal.[[4]](#cite_note-Chaurasia2013-4) In a newborn human baby the stomach will only be able to hold about 30 millilitres. The maximum stomach volume in adults is between 2 and 4 litres,[[6]](#cite_note-pmid9863573-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) although volumes of up to 15 litres have been observed in extreme circumstances.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Sections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Sections")]

"Cardia" redirects here. For the ancient Greek colony, see [Cardia (Thrace)](/wiki/Cardia_(Thrace) "Cardia (Thrace)"). Not to be confused with [Cardiac](/wiki/Cardiac "Cardiac").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/2414_Stomach.jpg/250px-2414_Stomach.jpg)](/wiki/File:2414_Stomach.jpg)

Diagram showing parts of the stomach

The human stomach can be divided into four sections, beginning at the cardia followed by the fundus, the body and the pylorus.[[9]](#cite_note-Tortora09-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Elsevier-10)

* The gastric **cardia** is where the contents of the esophagus empty from the [gastroesophageal sphincter](/wiki/Gastroesophageal_sphincter "Gastroesophageal sphincter") into the cardiac orifice, the opening into the gastric cardia.[[11]](#cite_note-SCHWARTZ2010-11)[[10]](#cite_note-Elsevier-10) A cardiac notch at the left of the cardiac orifice, marks the beginning of the [greater curvature](/wiki/Greater_curvature "Greater curvature") of the stomach. A horizontal line across from the cardiac notch gives the dome-shaped region called the fundus.[[10]](#cite_note-Elsevier-10) The cardia is a very small region of the stomach that surrounds the esophageal opening.[[10]](#cite_note-Elsevier-10)
* The **fundus** (from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") 'bottom') is formed in the upper curved part.[[12]](#cite_note-SEER2025-12)
* The **body** or **corpus** is the main, central region of the stomach.[[12]](#cite_note-SEER2025-12)
* The [pylorus](/wiki/Pylorus "Pylorus") (from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") 'gatekeeper') connects the stomach to the [duodenum](/wiki/Duodenum "Duodenum") at the [pyloric sphincter](/wiki/Pyloric_sphincter "Pyloric sphincter"). The [pyloric antrum](/wiki/Pyloric_antrum "Pyloric antrum") opens to the body of the stomach.[[12]](#cite_note-SEER2025-12)

The cardia is defined as the region following the "z-line" of the [gastroesophageal junction](/wiki/Gastroesophageal_junction "Gastroesophageal junction"), the point at which the [epithelium](/wiki/Epithelium "Epithelium") changes from [stratified squamous](/wiki/Stratified_squamous_epithelia "Stratified squamous epithelia") to [columnar](/wiki/Columnar "Columnar"). Near the cardia is the lower esophageal sphincter.[[11]](#cite_note-SCHWARTZ2010-11)

### Anatomical proximity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Anatomical proximity")]

The **stomach bed** refers to the structures upon which the stomach rests in mammals.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) These include the [tail of the pancreas](/wiki/Tail_of_the_pancreas "Tail of the pancreas"), [splenic artery](/wiki/Splenic_artery "Splenic artery"), left [kidney](/wiki/Kidney "Kidney"), left [suprarenal gland](/wiki/Suprarenal_gland "Suprarenal gland"), [transverse colon](/wiki/Transverse_colon "Transverse colon") and its [mesocolon](/wiki/Mesocolon "Mesocolon"), and the [left crus of diaphragm](/wiki/Left_crus_of_diaphragm "Left crus of diaphragm"), and the [left colic flexure](/wiki/Left_colic_flexure "Left colic flexure"). The term was introduced around 1896 by Philip Polson of the Catholic University School of Medicine, Dublin. However this was brought into disrepute by surgeon anatomist J Massey.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Blood supply

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Blood supply")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Stomach_blood_supply.svg/250px-Stomach_blood_supply.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Stomach_blood_supply.svg)

Schematic image of the blood supply to the human stomach: [left](/wiki/Left_gastric_artery "Left gastric artery") and [right gastric artery](/wiki/Right_gastric_artery "Right gastric artery"), [left](/wiki/Left_gastroepiploic_artery "Left gastroepiploic artery") and [right gastroepiploic artery](/wiki/Right_gastroepiploic_artery "Right gastroepiploic artery") and [short gastric arteries](/wiki/Short_gastric_arteries "Short gastric arteries")[[18]](#cite_note-Moore150-18)

The [lesser curvature](/wiki/Lesser_curvature "Lesser curvature") of the human stomach is supplied by the [right gastric artery](/wiki/Right_gastric_artery "Right gastric artery") inferiorly and the [left gastric artery](/wiki/Left_gastric_artery "Left gastric artery") superiorly, which also supplies the cardiac region. The greater curvature is supplied by the [right gastroepiploic artery](/wiki/Right_gastroepiploic_artery "Right gastroepiploic artery") inferiorly and the [left gastroepiploic artery](/wiki/Left_gastroepiploic_artery "Left gastroepiploic artery") superiorly. The fundus of the stomach, and also the upper portion of the greater curvature, is supplied by 5-7 [short gastric arteries](/wiki/Short_gastric_arteries "Short gastric arteries"), which arise from the splenic artery.[[4]](#cite_note-Chaurasia2013-4)

### Lymphatic drainage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Lymphatic drainage")]

The two sets of [gastric lymph nodes](/wiki/Gastric_lymph_nodes "Gastric lymph nodes") drain the stomach's tissue fluid into the lymphatic system through the [intestinal lymph trunk](/wiki/Intestinal_lymph_trunk "Intestinal lymph trunk"), to the [cisterna chyli](/wiki/Cisterna_chyli "Cisterna chyli").[[4]](#cite_note-Chaurasia2013-4)

### Microanatomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Microanatomy")]

#### Wall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Wall")]

Main article: [Gastrointestinal wall](/wiki/Gastrointestinal_wall "Gastrointestinal wall")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gastrointestinal_wall_of_human_stomach_hariadhi.svg/250px-Gastrointestinal_wall_of_human_stomach_hariadhi.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gastrointestinal_wall_of_human_stomach_hariadhi.svg)

The gastrointestinal wall of the human stomach

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Layers_of_the_GI_Tract_english.svg/250px-Layers_of_the_GI_Tract_english.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Layers_of_the_GI_Tract_english.svg)

Layers of the gastrointestinal wall of which the stomach is a dilated part

Like the other parts of the [gastrointestinal wall](/wiki/Gastrointestinal_wall "Gastrointestinal wall"), the human stomach wall from inner to outer, consists of a [mucosa](/wiki/Mucosa "Mucosa"), [submucosa](/wiki/Submucosa "Submucosa"), [muscular layer](/wiki/Muscular_layer "Muscular layer"), [subserosa](/wiki/Subserosa "Subserosa") and [serosa](/wiki/Serosa "Serosa").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The inner part of the stomach wall is the [gastric mucosa](/wiki/Gastric_mucosa "Gastric mucosa") a mucous membrane that forms the lining of the stomach. The membrane consists of an outer layer of [columnar epithelium](/wiki/Columnar_epithelium "Columnar epithelium"), a [lamina propria](/wiki/Lamina_propria "Lamina propria"), and a thin layer of [smooth muscle](/wiki/Smooth_muscle "Smooth muscle") called the [muscularis mucosa](/wiki/Muscularis_mucosa "Muscularis mucosa"). Beneath the mucosa lies the [submucosa](/wiki/Submucosa "Submucosa"), consisting of [fibrous connective tissue](/wiki/Fibrous_connective_tissue "Fibrous connective tissue").[[20]](#cite_note-20) [Meissner's plexus](/wiki/Meissner%27s_plexus "Meissner's plexus") is in this layer interior to the oblique muscle layer.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Outside of the submucosa lies the muscular layer. It consists of three layers of muscular fibres, with fibres lying at angles to each other. These are the inner oblique, middle circular, and outer longitudinal layers.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The presence of the inner oblique layer is distinct from other parts of the gastrointestinal tract, which do not possess this layer.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The stomach contains the thickest muscular layer consisting of three layers, thus maximum peristalsis occurs here.

* The *inner oblique layer:* This layer is responsible for creating the motion that churns and physically breaks down the food. It is the only layer of the three which is not seen in other parts of the [digestive system](/wiki/Digestive_system "Digestive system"). The antrum has thicker skin cells in its walls and performs more forceful contractions than the fundus.
* The *middle circular layer:* At this layer, the [pylorus](/wiki/Pylorus "Pylorus") is surrounded by a thick circular muscular wall, which is normally tonically constricted, forming a functional (if not anatomically discrete) [pyloric sphincter](/wiki/Pyloric_sphincter "Pyloric sphincter"), which controls the movement of [chyme](/wiki/Chyme "Chyme") into the [duodenum](/wiki/Duodenum "Duodenum"). This layer is concentric to the [longitudinal axis](/wiki/Anatomical_terms_of_location#Axes "Anatomical terms of location") of the stomach.
* The [myenteric plexus](/wiki/Myenteric_plexus "Myenteric plexus") (Auerbach's plexus) is found between the outer longitudinal and the middle circular layer and is responsible for the innervation of both (causing [peristalsis](/wiki/Peristalsis "Peristalsis") and mixing).

The *outer longitudinal layer* is responsible for moving the semi-digested food towards the pylorus of the stomach through muscular shortening.

To the outside of the muscular layer lies a [serosa](/wiki/Serosa "Serosa"), consisting of layers of connective tissue continuous with the [peritoneum](/wiki/Peritoneum "Peritoneum").

Smooth mucosa along the inside of the lesser curvature forms a passageway - the **gastric canal** that fast-tracks liquids entering the stomach, to the pylorus.[[10]](#cite_note-Elsevier-10)

#### Glands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Glands")]

Main article: [Gastric glands](/wiki/Gastric_glands "Gastric glands")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Illu_stomach_layers.jpg/250px-Illu_stomach_layers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Illu_stomach_layers.jpg)

Diagram showing gastric pits (13) gastric glands (12) lamina propria (10) epithelium (11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Histology_of_normal_fundic_mucosa.png/250px-Histology_of_normal_fundic_mucosa.png)](/wiki/File:Histology_of_normal_fundic_mucosa.png)

Histology of normal fundic mucosa. Fundic glands are simple, branched tubular glands that extend from the bottom of the gastric pits to the muscularis mucosae; the more distinctive cells are parietal cells. H&E stain.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Histology_of_normal_antral_mucosa.png/250px-Histology_of_normal_antral_mucosa.png)](/wiki/File:Histology_of_normal_antral_mucosa.png)

Histology of normal antral mucosa. Antral mucosa is formed by branched coiled tubular glands lined by secretory cells similar in appearance to the surface mucous cells. H&E stain.

The mucosa lining the stomach is lined with [gastric pits](/wiki/Gastric_pit "Gastric pit"), which receive [gastric juice](/wiki/Gastric_juice "Gastric juice"), secreted by between 2 and 7 [gastric glands](/wiki/Gastric_glands "Gastric glands").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Gastric juice is an acidic fluid containing [hydrochloric acid](/wiki/Hydrochloric_acid "Hydrochloric acid") and digestive enzymes.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The glands contains a number of cells, with the function of the glands changing depending on their position within the stomach.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Within the body and fundus of the stomach lie the *fundic glands*. In general, these glands are lined by column-shaped cells that secrete a protective layer of [mucus](/wiki/Mucus "Mucus") and [bicarbonate](/wiki/Bicarbonate "Bicarbonate"). Additional cells present include [parietal cells](/wiki/Parietal_cell "Parietal cell") that secrete hydrochloric acid and [intrinsic factor](/wiki/Intrinsic_factor "Intrinsic factor"), [chief cells](/wiki/Gastric_chief_cell "Gastric chief cell") that secrete [pepsinogen](/wiki/Pepsinogen "Pepsinogen") (this is a precursor to pepsin- the highly acidic environment converts the pepsinogen to pepsin), and neuroendocrine cells that secrete [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin").[[25]](#cite_note-Dorland777-25)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Glands differ where the stomach meets the esophagus and near the pylorus.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Near the [gastroesophageal junction](/wiki/Gastroesophageal_junction "Gastroesophageal junction") lie *cardiac glands*, which primarily secrete mucus.[[25]](#cite_note-Dorland777-25) They are fewer in number than the other gastric glands and are more shallowly positioned in the mucosa. There are two kinds - either simple [tubular glands](/wiki/Tubular_gland "Tubular gland") with short ducts or [compound racemose](/wiki/Compound_racemose_glands "Compound racemose glands") resembling the [duodenal](/wiki/Duodenal "Duodenal") [Brunner's glands](/wiki/Brunner%27s_glands "Brunner's glands").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Near the pylorus lie *pyloric glands* located in the antrum of the pylorus. They secrete mucus, as well as [gastrin](/wiki/Gastrin "Gastrin") produced by their [G cells](/wiki/G_cells "G cells").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Gene and protein expression

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Gene and protein expression")]

Further information: [Bioinformatics § Gene and protein expression](/wiki/Bioinformatics#Gene_and_protein_expression "Bioinformatics")

About 20,000 [protein-coding genes](/wiki/Protein-coding_genes "Protein-coding genes") are expressed in human cells and nearly 70% of these genes are expressed in the normal stomach.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) Just over 150 of these genes are more specifically expressed in the stomach compared to other organs, with only some 20 genes being highly specific. The corresponding specific proteins expressed in stomach are mainly involved in creating a suitable environment for handling the digestion of food for uptake of nutrients. Highly stomach-specific proteins include [gastrokine-1](/wiki/Gastrokine-1 "Gastrokine-1") expressed in the mucosa; [pepsinogen](/wiki/Pepsinogen "Pepsinogen") and [gastric lipase](/wiki/Gastric_lipase "Gastric lipase"), expressed in [gastric chief cells](/wiki/Gastric_chief_cell "Gastric chief cell"); and a gastric [ATPase](/wiki/Hydrogen_potassium_ATPase "Hydrogen potassium ATPase") and [gastric intrinsic factor](/wiki/Gastric_intrinsic_factor "Gastric intrinsic factor"), expressed in [parietal cells](/wiki/Parietal_cell "Parietal cell").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Development")]

In the early part of the [development of the human embryo](/wiki/Human_embryogenesis "Human embryogenesis"), the ventral part of the embryo abuts the [yolk sac](/wiki/Yolk_sac "Yolk sac"). During the third week of development, as the embryo grows, it begins to surround parts of the yolk sac. The enveloped portions form the basis for the adult gastrointestinal tract.[[31]](#cite_note-LARSEN2009-31) The sac is surrounded by a network of [vitelline arteries and veins](/wiki/Vitelline_arteries "Vitelline arteries"). Over time, these arteries consolidate into the three main arteries that supply the developing gastrointestinal tract: the [celiac artery](/wiki/Celiac_artery "Celiac artery"), [superior mesenteric artery](/wiki/Superior_mesenteric_artery "Superior mesenteric artery"), and [inferior mesenteric artery](/wiki/Inferior_mesenteric_artery "Inferior mesenteric artery"). The areas supplied by these arteries are used to define the [foregut](/wiki/Foregut "Foregut"), [midgut](/wiki/Midgut "Midgut"), and [hindgut](/wiki/Hindgut "Hindgut").[[31]](#cite_note-LARSEN2009-31) The surrounded sac becomes the primitive gut. Sections of this gut begin to differentiate into the organs of the gastrointestinal tract, and the esophagus, and stomach form from the foregut.[[31]](#cite_note-LARSEN2009-31)

As the stomach rotates during early development, the dorsal and ventral mesentery rotate with it; this rotation produces a space anterior to the expanding stomach called the greater sac, and a space posterior to the stomach called the lesser sac. After this rotation the dorsal mesentery thins and forms the greater [omentum](/wiki/Greater_omentum "Greater omentum"), which is attached to the greater curvature of the stomach. The ventral mesentery forms the lesser omentum, and is attached to the developing [liver](/wiki/Liver "Liver"). In the adult, these connective structures of omentum and mesentery form the [peritoneum](/wiki/Peritoneum "Peritoneum"), and act as an insulating and protective layer while also supplying organs with [blood](/wiki/Blood "Blood") and lymph vessels as well as nerves.[[32]](#cite_note-SADLER-32) Arterial supply to all these structures is from the [celiac trunk](/wiki/Celiac_trunk "Celiac trunk"), and venous drainage is by the [portal venous system](/wiki/Portal_venous_system "Portal venous system"). Lymph from these organs is drained to the prevertebral celiac nodes at the origin of the celiac artery from the [aorta](/wiki/Aorta "Aorta").

## Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Function")]

### Digestion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Digestion")]

Further information: [Human digestive system](/wiki/Human_digestive_system "Human digestive system")

See also: [Gastric acid](/wiki/Gastric_acid "Gastric acid")

In the [human digestive system](/wiki/Human_digestive_system "Human digestive system"), a [bolus](/wiki/Bolus_(digestion) "Bolus (digestion)") (a small rounded mass of [chewed up](/wiki/Mastication "Mastication") food) enters the stomach through the esophagus via the [lower esophageal sphincter](/wiki/Esophagus#Sphincters "Esophagus"). The stomach releases [proteases](/wiki/Proteases "Proteases") (protein-digesting [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") such as [pepsin](/wiki/Pepsin "Pepsin")), and [hydrochloric acid](/wiki/Hydrochloric_acid "Hydrochloric acid"), which kills or inhibits [bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") and provides the acidic [pH](/wiki/PH "PH") of 2 for the proteases to work. Food is churned by the stomach through [peristaltic](/wiki/Peristalsis "Peristalsis") muscular contractions of the wall – reducing the volume of the bolus, before looping around the fundus[[33]](#cite_note-33) and the [body of stomach](/wiki/Body_of_stomach "Body of stomach") as the boluses are converted into [chyme](/wiki/Chyme "Chyme") (partially digested food). Chyme slowly passes through the [pyloric sphincter](/wiki/Pyloric_sphincter "Pyloric sphincter") and into the [duodenum](/wiki/Duodenum "Duodenum") of the [small intestine](/wiki/Small_intestine "Small intestine"), where the extraction of nutrients begins.

[Gastric juice](/wiki/Gastric_juice "Gastric juice") in the stomach contains [pepsinogen](/wiki/Pepsinogen "Pepsinogen") and [gastric acid](/wiki/Gastric_acid "Gastric acid"), ([hydrochloric acid](/wiki/Hydrochloric_acid "Hydrochloric acid")) which activates this inactive form of enzyme into the active form, pepsin. Pepsin breaks down proteins into [polypeptides](/wiki/Polypeptides "Polypeptides").

#### Mechanical digestion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Mechanical digestion")]

Within a few moments after food enters the stomach, mixing waves begin to occur at intervals of approximately 20 seconds. A mixing wave is a unique type of [peristalsis](/wiki/Peristalsis "Peristalsis") that mixes and softens the food with gastric juices to create chyme. The initial mixing waves are relatively gentle, but these are followed by more intense waves, starting at the body of the stomach and increasing in force as they reach the pylorus.

The pylorus, which holds around 30 mL of chyme, acts as a filter, permitting only liquids and small food particles to pass through the mostly, but not fully, closed pyloric sphincter. In a process called [gastric emptying](/wiki/Gastric_emptying "Gastric emptying"), rhythmic mixing waves force about 3 mL of chyme at a time through the pyloric sphincter and into the duodenum. Release of a greater amount of chyme at one time would overwhelm the capacity of the small intestine to handle it. The rest of the chyme is pushed back into the body of the stomach, where it continues mixing. This process is repeated when the next mixing waves force more chyme into the duodenum.

Gastric emptying is regulated by both the stomach and the duodenum. The presence of chyme in the duodenum activates receptors that inhibit gastric secretion. This prevents additional chyme from being released by the stomach before the duodenum is ready to process it.[[34]](#cite_note-Openstax_Anatomy_&_Physiology_attribution-34)

#### Chemical digestion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Chemical digestion")]

The fundus stores both undigested food and gases that are released during the process of chemical digestion. Food may sit in the fundus of the stomach for a while before being mixed with the chyme. While the food is in the fundus, the digestive activities of [salivary amylase](/wiki/Salivary_amylase "Salivary amylase") continue until the food begins mixing with the acidic chyme. Ultimately, mixing waves incorporate this food with the chyme, the acidity of which inactivates salivary amylase and activates [lingual lipase](/wiki/Lingual_lipase "Lingual lipase"). Lingual lipase then begins breaking down triglycerides into free fatty acids, and mono- and diglycerides.

The breakdown of protein begins in the stomach through the actions of hydrochloric acid, and the enzyme [pepsin](/wiki/Pepsin "Pepsin").

The stomach can also produce [gastric lipase](/wiki/Gastric_lipase "Gastric lipase"), which can help digesting fat.

The contents of the stomach are completely emptied into the duodenum within two to four hours after the meal is eaten. Different types of food take different amounts of time to process. Foods heavy in carbohydrates empty fastest, followed by high-protein foods. Meals with a high triglyceride content remain in the stomach the longest. Since enzymes in the small intestine digest fats slowly, food can stay in the stomach for 6 hours or longer when the duodenum is processing fatty chyme. However, this is still a fraction of the 24 to 72 hours that full digestion typically takes from start to finish.[[34]](#cite_note-Openstax_Anatomy_&_Physiology_attribution-34)

### Absorption

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Absorption")]

Although the absorption in the human digestive system is mainly a function of the small intestine, some absorption of certain small molecules nevertheless does occur in the stomach through its lining. This includes:

* Water, if the body is [dehydrated](/wiki/Dehydration "Dehydration")
* Medication, such as [aspirin](/wiki/Aspirin "Aspirin")
* [Amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acids "Amino acids")[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* 10–20% of ingested [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol") (e.g. from alcoholic beverages)[[36]](#cite_note-intox-36)
* [Caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine")[[37]](#cite_note-37)
* To a small extent water-soluble [vitamins](/wiki/Vitamin "Vitamin") (most are absorbed in the small intestine)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

The [parietal cells](/wiki/Parietal_cell "Parietal cell") of the human stomach are responsible for producing [intrinsic factor](/wiki/Intrinsic_factor "Intrinsic factor"), which is necessary for the absorption of [vitamin B12](/wiki/Vitamin_B12 "Vitamin B12"). B12 is used in cellular metabolism and is necessary for the production of [red blood cells](/wiki/Red_blood_cell "Red blood cell"), and the functioning of the [nervous system](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system").

### Control of secretion and motility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Control of secretion and motility")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Stomach_emptying_into_duodenum.svg/960px-Stomach_emptying_into_duodenum.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Stomach_emptying_into_duodenum.svg)

Emptying of stomach chyme into the duodenum through the pyloric sphincter

Chyme from the stomach is slowly released into the [duodenum](/wiki/Duodenum "Duodenum") through coordinated [peristalsis](/wiki/Peristalsis "Peristalsis") and opening of the pyloric sphincter. The movement and the flow of chemicals into the stomach are controlled by both the [autonomic nervous system](/wiki/Autonomic_nervous_system "Autonomic nervous system") and by the various [digestive hormones](/wiki/Digestion#Digestive_hormones "Digestion") of the digestive system:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Gastrin](/wiki/Gastrin "Gastrin") | The hormone *gastrin* causes an increase in the secretion of HCl from the parietal cells and pepsinogen from chief cells in the stomach. It also causes increased motility in the stomach. Gastrin is released by [G cells](/wiki/G_cell "G cell") in the stomach in response to distension of the antrum and digestive products (especially large quantities of incompletely digested proteins). It is inhibited by a [pH](/wiki/PH "PH") normally less than 4(high acid), as well as the hormone [somatostatin](/wiki/Somatostatin "Somatostatin"). |
| [Cholecystokinin](/wiki/Cholecystokinin "Cholecystokinin") | *Cholecystokinin* (CCK) has most effect on the [gall bladder](/wiki/Gall_bladder "Gall bladder"), causing gall bladder contractions, but it also decreases gastric emptying and increases release of [pancreatic](/wiki/Pancreas "Pancreas") juice, which is alkaline and neutralizes the chyme. CCK is synthesized by I-cells in the mucosal epithelium of the small intestine. |
| [Secretin](/wiki/Secretin "Secretin") | In a different and rare manner, *secretin*, which has the most effects on the pancreas, also diminishes acid secretion in the stomach. Secretin is synthesized by [S-cells](/wiki/S_cell "S cell"), which are located in the duodenal mucosa as well as in the jejunal mucosa in smaller numbers. |
| [Gastric inhibitory polypeptide](/wiki/Gastric_inhibitory_polypeptide "Gastric inhibitory polypeptide") | *Gastric inhibitory polypeptide* (GIP) decreases both gastric acid release and motility. GIP is synthesized by K-cells, which are located in the duodenal and jejunal mucosa. |
| [Enteroglucagon](/wiki/Enteroglucagon "Enteroglucagon") | *Enteroglucagon* decreases both gastric acid and motility. |

Other than gastrin, these hormones all act to turn off the stomach action. This is in response to food products in the [liver](/wiki/Liver "Liver") and gall bladder, which have not yet been absorbed. The stomach needs to push food into the small intestine only when the intestine is not busy. While the intestine is full and still digesting food, the stomach acts as storage for food.

### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Other")]

Effects of EGF

[Epidermal growth factor](/wiki/Epidermal_growth_factor "Epidermal growth factor") (EGF) results in cellular proliferation, differentiation, and survival.[[39]](#cite_note-Herbst-39) EGF is a low-molecular-weight polypeptide first purified from the mouse submandibular gland, but since then found in many human tissues including the [submandibular gland](/wiki/Submandibular_gland "Submandibular gland"), and the [parotid gland](/wiki/Parotid_gland "Parotid gland"). Salivary EGF, which also seems to be regulated by dietary inorganic [iodine](/wiki/Iodine "Iodine"), also plays an important physiological role in the maintenance of oro-esophageal and gastric tissue integrity. The biological effects of salivary EGF include healing of oral and gastroesophageal ulcers, inhibition of gastric acid secretion, stimulation of DNA synthesis, and mucosal protection from intraluminal injurious factors such as gastric acid, bile acids, pepsin, and trypsin and from physical, chemical, and bacterial agents.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Stomach as nutrition sensor

The human stomach has receptors responsive to [sodium glutamate](/wiki/Sodium_glutamate "Sodium glutamate")[[41]](#cite_note-41) and this information is passed to the [lateral hypothalamus](/wiki/Lateral_hypothalamus "Lateral hypothalamus") and [limbic system](/wiki/Limbic_system "Limbic system") in the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") as a [palatability](/wiki/Palatability "Palatability") signal through the [vagus nerve](/wiki/Vagus_nerve "Vagus nerve").[[42]](#cite_note-42) The stomach can also sense, independently of tongue and oral taste receptors, [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose"),[[43]](#cite_note-Araujo-43) [carbohydrates](/wiki/Carbohydrate "Carbohydrate"),[[44]](#cite_note-Perez-44) [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein"),[[44]](#cite_note-Perez-44) and [fats](/wiki/Fat "Fat").[[45]](#cite_note-45) This allows the brain to link [nutritional](/wiki/Nutritional "Nutritional") value of foods to their tastes.[[43]](#cite_note-Araujo-43)

Thyrogastric syndrome

This syndrome defines the association between thyroid disease and chronic gastritis, which was first described in the 1960s.[[46]](#cite_note-46) This term was coined also to indicate the presence of thyroid autoantibodies or autoimmune thyroid disease in patients with pernicious anemia, a late clinical stage of atrophic gastritis.[[47]](#cite_note-47) In 1993, a more complete investigation on the stomach and thyroid was published,[[48]](#cite_note-48) reporting that the thyroid is, embryogenetically and phylogenetically, derived from a primitive stomach, and that the thyroid cells, such as primitive gastroenteric cells, migrated and specialized in uptake of iodide and in storage and elaboration of iodine compounds during vertebrate evolution. In fact, the stomach and thyroid share iodine-concentrating ability and many morphological and functional similarities, such as cell polarity and apical microvilli, similar organ-specific antigens and associated autoimmune diseases, secretion of glycoproteins (thyroglobulin and mucin) and peptide hormones, the digesting and readsorbing ability, and lastly, similar ability to form iodotyrosines by peroxidase activity, where iodide acts as an electron donor in the presence of H2O2. In the following years, many researchers published reviews about this syndrome.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

### Diseases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Diseases")]

Main article: [Stomach disease](/wiki/Stomach_disease "Stomach disease")

A [series of radiographs](/wiki/Upper_gastrointestinal_series "Upper gastrointestinal series") can be used to examine the stomach for various disorders. This will often include the use of a [barium swallow](/wiki/Upper_gastrointestinal_series "Upper gastrointestinal series"). Another method of examination of the stomach is the use of an [endoscope](/wiki/Endoscopy "Endoscopy"). A [gastric emptying study](/wiki/Gastric_emptying_study "Gastric emptying study") is considered the gold standard to assess the gastric emptying rate.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

A large number of studies have indicated that most cases of [peptic ulcers](/wiki/Peptic_ulcer "Peptic ulcer"), and [gastritis](/wiki/Gastritis "Gastritis"), in humans are caused by *[Helicobacter pylori](/wiki/Helicobacter_pylori "Helicobacter pylori")* infection, and an association has been seen with the development of [stomach cancer](/wiki/Stomach_cancer "Stomach cancer").[[51]](#cite_note-Brown-51)

A [stomach rumble](/wiki/Stomach_rumble "Stomach rumble") is actually noise from the intestines.

### Surgery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Surgery")]

In humans, many [bariatric surgery](/wiki/Bariatric_surgery "Bariatric surgery") procedures involve the stomach, in order to lose weight. A [gastric band](/wiki/Adjustable_gastric_band "Adjustable gastric band") may be placed around the cardia area, which can adjust to limit intake. The [anatomy of the stomach may be modified](/wiki/Sleeve_gastrectomy "Sleeve gastrectomy"), or the stomach may be [bypassed entirely](/wiki/Gastric_bypass_surgery "Gastric bypass surgery").

Surgical removal of the stomach is called a [gastrectomy](/wiki/Gastrectomy "Gastrectomy"), and removal of the cardia area is a called a **cardiectomy**. "Cardiectomy" is a term that is also used to describe the removal of the [heart](/wiki/Heart "Heart").[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-BARLOW-53)[[54]](#cite_note-MELTZER-54) A gastrectomy may be carried out because of gastric cancer or severe perforation of the stomach wall.

[Fundoplication](/wiki/Nissen_fundoplication "Nissen fundoplication") is stomach surgery in which the fundus is wrapped around the lower esophagus and stitched into place. It is used to treat [gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)](/wiki/Gastroesophageal_reflux_disease "Gastroesophageal reflux disease").[[55]](#cite_note-Minjarez06-55)

### Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word *stomach* is derived from [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") *stomachos* (στόμαχος), ultimately from *stoma* (στόμα) 'mouth'.[[56]](#cite_note-56) *Gastro-* and *gastric* (meaning 'related to the stomach') are both derived from Greek *gaster* (γαστήρ) 'belly'.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## Other animals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stomach&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Other animals")]

Although the precise shape and size of the stomach varies widely among different vertebrates, the relative positions of the esophageal and duodenal openings remain relatively constant. As a result, the organ always curves somewhat to the left before curving back to meet the pyloric sphincter. However, [lampreys](/wiki/Lamprey "Lamprey"), [hagfishes](/wiki/Hagfish "Hagfish"), [chimaeras](/wiki/Chimaera "Chimaera"), [lungfishes](/wiki/Lungfish "Lungfish"), and some [teleost](/wiki/Teleost "Teleost") fish have no stomach at all, with the esophagus opening directly into the intestine. These animals all consume diets that require little storage of food, no predigestion with gastric juices, or both.[[60]](#cite_note-VB-60)

| Comparison of stomach glandular regions from several mammalian species. Frequency of glands may vary more smoothly between regions than is diagrammed here. Asterisk (ruminant) represents the omasum, which is absent in [Tylopoda](/wiki/Tylopoda "Tylopoda"). (Tylopoda also have some cardiac glands opening onto ventral [reticulum](/wiki/Reticulum_(anatomy) "Reticulum (anatomy)") and [rumen](/wiki/Rumen "Rumen").[[61]](#cite_note-61)) Many other variations exist among the mammals.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) | Yellow  [Esophagus](/wiki/Oesophagus "Oesophagus")  Green  [Esophageal (nonglandular) region](/w/index.php?title=Oesophageal_(nonglandular)_region&action=edit&redlink=1 "Oesophageal (nonglandular) region (page does not exist)").[[64]](#cite_note-APFA-64)  Purple  [Cardiac gland region](/w/index.php?title=Cardiac_gland_region&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cardiac gland region (page does not exist)").[[64]](#cite_note-APFA-64)  Red  [Fundic gland region](/w/index.php?title=Fundic_gland_region&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fundic gland region (page does not exist)").[[64]](#cite_note-APFA-64)  Blue  [Pyloric gland region](/w/index.php?title=Pyloric_gland_region&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pyloric gland region (page does not exist)").[[64]](#cite_note-APFA-64)  Dark blue  [Duodenum](/wiki/Duodenum "Duodenum") |

The gastric lining is usually divided into two regions, an anterior portion lined by fundic glands and a posterior portion lined with pyloric glands. Cardiac glands are unique to [mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal"), and even then are absent in a number of species. The distributions of these glands vary between species, and do not always correspond with the same regions as in humans. Furthermore, in many non-human mammals, a portion of the stomach anterior to the cardiac glands is lined with epithelium essentially identical to that of the esophagus. [Ruminants](/wiki/Ruminant "Ruminant"), in particular, have a complex four-chambered stomach. The first three chambers ([rumen](/wiki/Rumen "Rumen"), [reticulum](/wiki/Reticulum_(anatomy) "Reticulum (anatomy)"), and [omasum](/wiki/Omasum "Omasum")) are all lined with esophageal mucosa,[[60]](#cite_note-VB-60) while the final chamber functions like a [monogastric](/wiki/Monogastric "Monogastric") stomach, which is called the [abomasum](/wiki/Abomasum "Abomasum").

In [birds](/wiki/Bird "Bird") and [crocodilians](/wiki/Crocodilian "Crocodilian"), the stomach is divided into two regions. Anteriorly is a narrow tubular region, the [proventriculus](/wiki/Proventriculus "Proventriculus"), lined by fundic glands, and connecting the true stomach to the [crop](/wiki/Crop_(anatomy) "Crop (anatomy)"). Beyond lies the powerful muscular [gizzard](/wiki/Gizzard "Gizzard"), lined by pyloric glands, and, in some species, containing stones that the animal swallows to help grind up food.[[60]](#cite_note-VB-60)

In [insects](/wiki/Insect "Insect"), there is also a crop. The insect stomach is called the [midgut](/wiki/Insect#Midgut "Insect").

Information about the stomach in [echinoderms](/wiki/Echinoderm "Echinoderm") or [molluscs](/wiki/Mollusc "Mollusc") can be found under the respective articles.
